title: Generalized chord tone selection: root through 13th
description:
Generalize the drill so the user defines which chord tones to cover, rather than the current hardcoded 3rd-then-7th. The 3rd and 7th were a starting point; this issue makes tone selection fully configurable.

Tone set: root, _3rd, _5th, _7th, _9th, _11th, _13th.

Design considerations:
- User selects any combination of tones to drill (e.g. 3rd only, 3rd + 7th, root + 3rd + 7th + 9th, etc.)
- UI: Material 3 multi-select toggle group (chip style) on the Setup screen; at least one tone must be selected
- Tap order follows the selected set in normal/reverse sequence
- Engine interface already supports additional tone roles — wire up the remaining ones
- Personal best history keyed by tone selection alongside progressionId and drillMode
- Engine must correctly spell tones per key
- Selection lives on the Setup screen and is sticky

title: Fretboard drill: user-selectable string scope
description:
Allow the user to limit which guitar strings are included when fret positions are displayed or tapped during fretboard drills. For example, a player focused on comping may only want to drill strings 1-4.

This affects both the fret dot display and (in fretboard input mode) the tappable target positions.

Design considerations:
- Setting lives on the Setup screen and is sticky (persisted alongside other setup choices)
- Engine/FretboardLocator filters positions to the selected string range before surfacing them
- Validation: selected strings must collectively cover all 12 semitones (at least one fret position per semitone). The Setup screen shows a live visual indicator when this constraint is not met; the drill cannot be started until it is satisfied.
- Selector UI TBD — to be fleshed out during feature planning

title: Weakest chord drill mode — pull from heatmap bottom performers
description:
Add a drill mode that automatically selects the user's worst-performing chords from heatmap data as the drill set. Instead of picking a progression, the app identifies the chords the user scores lowest on and builds a targeted drill session around them.

This closes the feedback loop between the heatmap and the drill — the heatmap identifies weaknesses, this mode acts on them.

Design considerations:
- Weakest mode appears as a chord-source option on the Setup screen, alongside the existing progression selector
- Requires per-chord performance data from the heatmap to be queryable (verify data model supports this)
- Need a threshold or count parameter: e.g. 'drill my worst N chords' or 'drill chords below X score'
- Should integrate with existing DrillMode enum and scoring system
- If heatmap data is insufficient (new user, no history), need a fallback or onboarding message
- Personal best history keyed separately from progression-based drills

title: Fully random chord drill mode
description:
Add a drill mode that generates a random set of chords rather than requiring the user to select a progression. The user selects a key (tonic + mode) and a chord count on the Setup screen, and the engine picks random chord qualities to drill.

This removes the setup friction of choosing a progression and gives players infinite variety. Useful for advanced players who want unpredictable practice.

Design considerations:
- Random mode appears as a chord-source option on the Setup screen, alongside the existing progression selector
- Random pool should cover all chord qualities the engine supports
- Should integrate with existing DrillMode enum and scoring/personal best system
- Keyed separately from progression-based drills in personal best history (no progressionId — use a sentinel or a dedicated key type)
- Deduplication rule (distinct chords) still applies

title: Flash pause visual polish: grey buttons, hide click-circles during fret display
description:
During the flash pause after a correct tap, there is no clear visual indicator that input is blocked. This issue implements consistent visual feedback across both input modes.

**Button mode:**
- During flash pause: note buttons go grey (instead of default color), timer freezes
- After pause ends: buttons return to normal color, fret markers disappear (primary 'go' signal), timer resumes

**Fretboard mode:**
- Buttons are ALWAYS grey — they are never the input surface in this mode
- During flash pause: click-circles (tap target hints) disappear from fretboard, fret markers appear, timer freezes
- After pause ends: fret markers disappear, click-circles reappear, timer resumes

The disappearance of fret markers is the primary signal to the user that input is re-enabled, backed by the timer unfreezing and (in button mode) buttons returning to color.

title: Sticky setup choices — persist tonic/progression/mode/input across sessions
description: Setup screen resets to hardcoded defaults on every open. Persist selections via a single SetupPrefs data class in DataStore (JSON). Also renames Start Drill button to Chord Drill. Files: SettingsRepository.kt, SetupViewModel.kt, SetupScreen.kt, AppNavigation.kt.
design:
# Feature: Sticky Setup Choices

## What this feature does

The Setup screen currently resets to hardcoded defaults every time it is opened. This feature persists the user's last selections so they are pre-populated on every subsequent visit, including after the app is killed and relaunched. It also renames the bottom action button from "Start Drill" to "Chord Drill".

## Design principle

All setup selections are captured in a single `@Serializable` data class (`SetupPrefs`) and written to DataStore as one JSON string whenever any selection changes. Adding a new selection in future requires: add a field with a default to `SetupPrefs`, include it in `persistPrefs()`. Nothing else.

Room is not used here — it is designed for collections of rows. A single-row config record belongs in DataStore.

`kotlinx.serialization` is already a project dependency (used in `DrillApplication.kt`).

---

## Scope

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `app/.../data/repository/SettingsRepository.kt` | Add `SetupPrefs` data class, one DataStore key, one Flow, one setter |
| `app/.../ui/viewmodel/SetupViewModel.kt` | Accept `SettingsRepository`; load `SetupPrefs` on init; write `SetupPrefs` on every selection change |
| `app/.../ui/screen/SetupScreen.kt` | Rename button label |
| `app/.../ui/navigation/AppNavigation.kt` | Pass `app.settingsRepository` to `SetupViewModel.Factory` |

Engine module is not touched.

---

## 1. SettingsRepository.kt

### Add `SetupPrefs` data class

Add this near the top of the file, before the `SettingsRepository` class declaration:

```kotlin
@Serializable
data class SetupPrefs(
    val tonic: String = TONIC_NAMES.first(),
    val progressionId: Long? = null,
    val drillMode: String = DrillMode.Normal.name,
    val inputMode: String = DrillInputMode.Buttons.name,
)
```

Default values are the fallback for first launch (no saved data) and for any field not present in an older saved JSON blob — `kotlinx.serialization` fills missing fields with their defaults automatically, so adding a field in future requires no migration.

Enum values are stored as their `.name` strings (not ordinals) so they survive reordering.

### Add one DataStore key, one Flow, one setter

Add to the `companion object`:
```kotlin
private val SETUP_PREFS = stringPreferencesKey("setup_prefs")
```

Add to the `SettingsRepository` class body (after the existing `correctDisplayMs` entries):
```kotlin
val setupPrefs: Flow<SetupPrefs> = dataStore.data.map { prefs ->
    prefs[SETUP_PREFS]
        ?.let { runCatching { Json.decodeFromString<SetupPrefs>(it) }.getOrNull() }
        ?: SetupPrefs()
}

suspend fun setSetupPrefs(prefs: SetupPrefs) {
    dataStore.edit { it[SETUP_PREFS] = Json.encodeToString(prefs) }
}
```

`runCatching` ensures a corrupted or unreadable JSON blob silently falls back to defaults rather than crashing.

### New imports needed in SettingsRepository.kt:
```kotlin
import com.lnkranch.yaga.domain.DrillInputMode
import com.lnkranch.yaga.domain.DrillMode
import com.lnkranch.yaga.domain.TONIC_NAMES
import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
import kotlinx.serialization.encodeToString
import kotlinx.serialization.json.Json
import kotlinx.serialization.decodeFromString
```

---

## 2. SetupViewModel.kt

### Constructor change

Add `SettingsRepository` as a second constructor parameter:
```kotlin
class SetupViewModel(
    repository: DrillRepository,
    private val settings: SettingsRepository,
) : ViewModel() {
```

### Initialization — load saved prefs

Add an `init` block that reads `SetupPrefs` once and initialises the StateFlows. Tonic, drill mode, and input mode are applied immediately. Progression ID requires validation against the loaded progressions list because the saved ID may refer to a deleted progression.

```kotlin
init {
    viewModelScope.launch {
        val saved = settings.setupPrefs.first()

        _selectedTonic.value     = saved.tonic
        _selectedDrillMode.value = runCatching { DrillMode.valueOf(saved.drillMode) }
                                       .getOrElse { DrillMode.Normal }
        _selectedInputMode.value = runCatching { DrillInputMode.valueOf(saved.inputMode) }
                                       .getOrElse { DrillInputMode.Buttons }

        val savedId = saved.progressionId ?: return@launch
        progressions
            .filter { it.isNotEmpty() }
            .take(1)
            .collect { list ->
                if (list.any { it.id == savedId }) {
                    _selectedProgressionId.value = savedId
                }
                // If the progression was deleted, leave as null — user must pick again
            }
    }
}
```

The `.filter { it.isNotEmpty() }.take(1)` pattern waits for Room to emit a non-empty list (skipping the initial empty emission), checks once, then stops collecting.

### Single persist helper

Add one private method that snapshots all current state into a `SetupPrefs` and writes it. All `selectX()` methods call this — there is no per-field persistence logic:

```kotlin
private fun persistPrefs() {
    viewModelScope.launch {
        settings.setSetupPrefs(
            SetupPrefs(
                tonic         = _selectedTonic.value,
                progressionId = _selectedProgressionId.value,
                drillMode     = _selectedDrillMode.value.name,
                inputMode     = _selectedInputMode.value.name,
            )
        )
    }
}
```

**To add a new sticky selection in future:** add a field with a default to `SetupPrefs`, apply it in `init`, and include it in `persistPrefs()`. No other changes required anywhere.

### Update selection methods

```kotlin
fun selectTonic(tonic: String) {
    _selectedTonic.value = tonic
    persistPrefs()
}

fun selectProgression(id: Long) {
    _selectedProgressionId.value = id
    persistPrefs()
}

fun selectDrillMode(mode: DrillMode) {
    _selectedDrillMode.value = mode
    persistPrefs()
}

fun selectInputMode(mode: DrillInputMode) {
    _selectedInputMode.value = mode
    persistPrefs()
}
```

### Update Factory

```kotlin
fun Factory(repository: DrillRepository, settings: SettingsRepository) =
    object : ViewModelProvider.Factory {
        override fun <T : ViewModel> create(modelClass: Class<T>): T {
            @Suppress("UNCHECKED_CAST")
            return SetupViewModel(repository, settings) as T
        }
    }
```

### New imports needed in SetupViewModel.kt:
```kotlin
import com.lnkranch.yaga.data.repository.SettingsRepository
import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.filter
import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.first
import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.take
```

---

## 3. SetupScreen.kt

Single line change — rename the button label (line 133):

```kotlin
// Before
Text("Start Drill")

// After
Text("Chord Drill")
```

---

## 4. AppNavigation.kt

Find the `composable("setup")` route. Update the `SetupViewModel.Factory` call:

```kotlin
// Before
factory = SetupViewModel.Factory(app.repository)

// After
factory = SetupViewModel.Factory(app.repository, app.settingsRepository)
```

---

## Edge cases

**Deleted progression:** If the saved `progressionId` no longer exists in the progressions list, the selection stays `null` and the "Chord Drill" button remains disabled. The user must pick a progression before drilling. Silently selecting a different progression would be worse.

**First launch:** All DataStore keys are absent. `setupPrefs` returns `SetupPrefs()` (all defaults), which matches the current hardcoded behaviour exactly.

**Corrupted JSON:** `runCatching { Json.decodeFromString(...) }.getOrNull()` returns `null`, which triggers the `?: SetupPrefs()` fallback. No crash.

**Adding a new selection later:** Add a field with a default to `SetupPrefs`, apply it in `init`, include it in `persistPrefs()`. The DataStore JSON blob from before the change simply won't have that key — `kotlinx.serialization` fills in the default. No migration needed.

---

## What this feature does NOT change

- The Settings screen (playing position, correct display time) — unaffected.
- The DrillViewModel, drill logic, scoring, heatmap — unaffected.
- The engine module — untouched.
- Navigation routes or argument structure — unaffected.

title: Practice statistics dashboard — zoomable history with reset markers
description:
Add a statistics view showing overall practice health over time. The heatmap shows where the user is weak; this shows whether they are improving.

Core visualization: a zoomable histogram of practice activity and score trends over time (similar to chess.com game history). The user can zoom to different time spans (week / month / year / all-time). Vertical marker lines indicate data reset events, so trend breaks are explained rather than misleading.

Summary stats: total sessions, total practice time, average score trend, personal best.

Design considerations:
- Accessible from the Home screen alongside Setup and Heatmap
- Data reset events are recorded with a timestamp so they can be rendered as vertical markers on the timeline
- Zooming/panning the histogram is the primary interaction; keep the implementation pragmatic (Android Canvas or a charting library)
- Pairs with yaga-iqx (session history) — that issue covers the data model; this issue covers the visualization layer

title: Daily practice reminders — push notification for habit building
description:
Add an opt-in daily reminder notification to encourage consistent practice. User sets a preferred time; the app sends a push notification if they haven't drilled that day.

Design considerations:
- Opt-in only, configured in Settings
- Requires notification permission (runtime request)
- Should suppress if a session has already been completed that day
- Standard Android local notification — no server required

title: Session history — review past drill scores over time
description:
Allow the user to browse their past drill sessions, not just personal bests. A history view shows scores, accuracy, and date for each completed session, giving a sense of progress over time.

Design considerations:
- Session history list grouped by progression and key
- Shows score, accuracy, mistap count, and date per session
- Pairs naturally with the heatmap as a complementary feedback view
- Data model must already capture per-session data — verify before implementing

title: Pause sometimes produces garbled timer readings
description:
After pausing and resuming, the session timer occasionally displays incorrect or garbled values. Intermittent — exact repro steps not yet identified.

Likely related to the pause time accounting logic in DrillViewModel (totalPausedMs / pauseStartMs calculations), possibly a race condition between the auto-pause (flash display) and user-initiated pause.

title: Guitar tuner submode — USB DI pitch detection utility
description:
Add a guitar tuner accessible within the app, using USB DI audio input. Displays the detected pitch and deviation from the nearest chromatic note so the user can tune before drilling.

This is a natural companion to the USB DI input mode and shares the same pitch detection and USB audio infrastructure.

Design considerations:
- Standard chromatic tuner display (note name + cents sharp/flat); UI is custom Canvas — needle or bar indicator
- Entry point is the Home screen, alongside the existing Setup and Heatmap buttons
- Candidate library: TarsosDSP (Java/Android, YIN/MPM pitch detection algorithms, standard for Android tuner apps)
- USB audio host routing will need to be built separately before TarsosDSP can consume it — this is the shared infrastructure with yaga-52w
- Shares USB audio host setup with the play-tone input mode

title: Play-tone input mode — USB DI pitch detection as drill input
description:
Add an input mode where the user plays the answer on their instrument via USB DI (direct input through a USB audio interface). The app detects the pitch and routes it through the existing tap() scoring logic as if it were a button tap.

This is the audio input subsystem referenced in the architecture. The tap logic is already designed to be swappable — this issue implements the USB audio path.

Design considerations:
- Use Android USB audio host mode to receive audio from a connected USB audio interface
- Pitch detection should identify the played note's semitone and route through existing tap() logic
- Needs USB host permission handling and device connection/disconnection lifecycle
- Should degrade gracefully when no USB audio device is connected (fallback prompt or mode switch)
- Monophonic detection sufficient for MVP (single note at a time)
- Timer behavior during detection window needs definition (pause while listening, or run continuously?)

title: Setup: empty progression list has no recovery path
description: Start button only grays out when no progression is selected, but users can reach a stuck state with an empty list. Add an empty state message and a primary Build Progression button inline in the progression list.

title: Local backup and export of heatmap and session data
description:
Allow the user to export their heatmap and session history to a local file, and restore from it. No cloud sync — local only. Protects against data loss from device wipe or reinstall.

Design considerations:
- Export to a standard format (JSON or CSV) to a user-chosen location via Android file picker
- Import from the same format with a confirm step before overwriting
- Accessible from Settings
- Should cover: heatmap scores, session history, personal bests

title: New user onboarding — tutorial for drill mechanics
description:
First-time users have no guidance on how the drill works — tapping the 3rd then 7th, what normal vs reverse mode means, how the fretboard display relates to their instrument, etc. Add an onboarding flow that explains the core mechanic.

Design considerations:
- One-time flow triggered on first launch, skippable
- Should cover: what chord tones are, how to read the drill screen, tap order (normal vs reverse), and fretboard display
- Re-accessible from the About screen or Settings for users who skipped it

title: Refine app icon and integrate into UI (splash screen, about)
description:
The app icon needs polish and should appear within the app itself, not just on the launcher.

Scope:
- Refine the icon asset (higher quality / final design)
- Add a brief startup splash screen featuring the icon (use Android SplashScreen API — the modern approach, not a legacy splash Activity)
- Show the icon on the About screen
- Evaluate other natural placements (e.g. empty states, onboarding)

Design note: splash screen should be minimal and fast — icon on a solid background, no animation required unless it feels right.

title: Add About item to Settings screen
description:
Standard Android UX places app info under Settings. Add an About entry at the bottom of the Settings screen that displays:

- App name and version (versionName from BuildConfig)
- Developer / company name
- Copyright notice
- Open source licenses (tappable, links to license list)
- Privacy policy link (if applicable)

Follow Material 3 conventions: tapping the About row opens a dedicated About screen or modal. Version string should be human-readable (e.g. 'Version 1.0.0').

title: Drill/Summary: replace generic error state with retry action
description: Failed to load session with no retry leaves users stranded. Add a Try Again button that reloads the drill, and log enough state to show what went wrong.

title: Settings: add Reset to defaults option
description: No way to recover original values after tuning settings (e.g. display time set to 0.1s). Add a Reset to defaults action in the overflow menu or a small Reset text button near the setting.

title: Improve Heatmap empty state copy
description: Current text: No drill data yet. Complete a session to see your heatmap. Replace with something more contextual and motivating, e.g. Nothing here yet — finish a drill to see which chords need work.

title: Move Heatmap out of Home drill card
description: Chord Drill card has Start and Heatmap side by side, making Heatmap look like a next step in the drill flow. Move Heatmap to a text link below the card or surface it from Summary screen instead.

title: Heatmap: add secondary encoding for color-blind accessibility
description: Green-to-red gradient is invisible to ~8% of male users. Add a secondary encoding alongside color: text badge (fast/medium/slow) or border thickness variation so color is not the only signal.

title: Extract hard-coded accent colors into DrillColors object
description: Semantic colors (green 0xFF4CAF50, 0xFF2E7D32, 0xFF1A3A5C) are hard-coded in FretboardDisplay and feedback system. Collect them in a DrillColors object so they are maintainable in one place.

title: Progression Builder: document tap-to-remove and note missing drag-to-reorder
description: Help text for tap-to-remove chips only appears after chords are added. Users have no affordance for drag-to-reorder. Ensure help text is always visible; drag-to-reorder is a v2 builder concern.

title: Rename Play Again / Back to Setup for clarity
description: Both buttons go to Setup, making the distinction unclear. Rename to Drill Again (same progression pre-selected) and Change Progression (or Setup), so users can tell them apart without reading both.

title: Replace invisible long-press delete on Heatmap rows
description: No affordance signals that long-pressing a chord row deletes it. Options: swipe-to-reveal delete (standard Android pattern), per-row deletion in the overflow menu as Remove chord, or first-visit hint.

title: Explain button shuffle to user
description: Button positions shuffle each chord but there is no UI signal this is intentional. Add a one-time tooltip or onboarding hint so users understand spatial memory failing is by design, not a bug. Pairs with shuffle animation feature.
